coredata.dc:description: The increasing demand for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) to support the global efforts in reducing carbon emissions have also made necessary the search for more efficient battery recycling technologies to prevent the depletion of raw materials. Pyrolysis, flotation and leaching are potential recycling unit processes that have recently drawn the attention as pretreatment, separation and dissolution methods in processes aimed at the recovery of materials from spent batteries. However, a study which systematically investigates the advantages and limitations resulting from the integration of these operations is still missing. In this work, an integrated experimental approach was applied to evaluate the recycling efficiency of pyrolysis, flotation and leaching together with their various combinations for the treatment of an industrially produced black mass. The results show that pyrolysis decreased the presence of cathode material in the graphite concentrate, increasing graphite purity from 60 to 80 %, although with a negative impact on graphite recovery from 60 to 50 %. Furthermore, the use of pyrolysis increased the leaching yield of metals from 40 to above 80 %. Additionally, the leaching yields of Ni and Co were further increased by nearly 5 % when flotation was applied, even without pyrolysis. With the combination of pyrolysis and flotation prior to leaching, an increased dissolution yield of metals from black mass was found for all the metals, particularly Ni (from 13.7 g/L to 19.8 g/L). The sole exception to this trend was Li, likely due to its dissolution in the flotation water. A rationalized explanation of this behavior is presented, supported by the characterization of the feed and product materials obtained under each different scenario. This work thus demonstrates that properly designed recycling stages can have a tangible impact in the process efficiency.

head.ce:title: An integrated study on pyrolysis, flotation, and leaching for optimized recycling of industrial battery materials

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Metallurgical Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering, Aalto University, Espoo 02150, Finland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increasing demand for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) to support the global efforts in reducing carbon emissions have also made necessary the search for more efficient battery recycling technologies to prevent the depletion of raw materials. Pyrolysis, flotation and leaching are potential recycling unit processes that have recently drawn the attention as pretreatment, separation and dissolution methods in processes aimed at the recovery of materials from spent batteries. However, a study which systematically investigates the advantages and limitations resulting from the integration of these operations is still missing. In this work, an integrated experimental approach was applied to evaluate the recycling efficiency of pyrolysis, flotation and leaching together with their various combinations for the treatment of an industrially produced black mass. The results show that pyrolysis decreased the presence of cathode material in the graphite concentrate, increasing graphite purity from 60 to 80 %, although with a negative impact on graphite recovery from 60 to 50 %. Furthermore, the use of pyrolysis increased the leaching yield of metals from 40 to above 80 %. Additionally, the leaching yields of Ni and Co were further increased by nearly 5 % when flotation was applied, even without pyrolysis. With the combination of pyrolysis and flotation prior to leaching, an increased dissolution yield of metals from black mass was found for all the metals, particularly Ni (from 13.7 g/L to 19.8 g/L). The sole exception to this trend was Li, likely due to its dissolution in the flotation water. A rationalized explanation of this behavior is presented, supported by the characterization of the feed and product materials obtained under each different scenario. This work thus demonstrates that properly designed recycling stages can have a tangible impact in the process efficiency.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are currently the most widely used energy storage devices found in consumer electronics, electric vehicles and grid energy storage systems due to their performance properties such as low self-discharge capacity and high energy density. (Brückner et al., 2020) Particularly due to their use in renewable energy applications and rapid development of electric vehicle (EV) technologies. LIBs have become an essential component in the efforts to decrease fossil fuels consumption to reduce climate emissions. (IEA. Global Critical Minerals Outlook, 2024; IEA, 2020).

A typical LIB consists of anode and cathode active particles, current collectors, electrolyte, separator, binder and outer casing. (Srinivasan et al., 2025) The cathode material usually consists of lithium metal oxides (LMOs) such as lithium cobalt oxide (LCO), lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxides (NMC) or lithium iron phosphate (LFP), coated on an Al foil current collector with the aid of a polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) binder. The most common anode material used commercially is graphite, coated on Cu foil current collector with a water soluble binder composed of styrene butadiene rubber (SBR)/carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) composites. (Koech et al., 2024; Chen et al., 2024) Anode and cathode fractions are separated by a porous polymeric membrane separator that allows the transfer of lithium ions through an electrolyte made of lithium salts and organic carbonates. (Lingappan et al., 2023; Kainat et al., 2024).

The rich material content and increasing demand for LIBs has driven some of their component raw materials to be classified as critical in several geographical areas. For example, the European Commission has classified graphite, Li, Mn, and Co as critical raw materials (CRMs). (European Commission. Study on the Critical Raw Materials for the EU, 2023) Although Cu and Ni do not meet the requirements to be considered CRMs, they are included in the special classification of strategic raw materials. (European Commission. Study on the Critical Raw Materials for the EU, 2023) To meet the increasing demand for CRMs for batteries while reducing the depletion of natural resources and the environmental impact of extractive activities, their circularity through efficient re-use, re-purposing and recycling has become necessary. (Zanoletti et al., 2024).

In an effort to overcome the limitations of state-of-the-art LIB recycling methods, hydrometallurgical processes have become widely studied to recover metals such as Co, Mn, Li, and Ni from spent cathode material with various degrees of success. (Raj et al., 2022; Bae and Kim, 2021) The advantages of hydrometallurgical processes are claimed to be high recoveries of valuable metals, lower temperature requirements compared to pyrometallurgical routes, and relatively simple scalability. (Yu et al., 2022) However, hydrometallurgical processes usually require a more thorough mechanical pretreatment to separate steel casing, electrolyte, separator, Al and Cu foil. After the pretreatment, a powder called “black mass” consisting of a solid mixture of anode and cathode active materials with other minor impurities (e.g., Al, Fe and Cu) is produced. (Milicevic Neumann et al., 2024) The metals present in the black mass can be leached using properly designed solvents. The dissolved metals can then be individually recovered from the pregnant solution using a series of processes such as solvent extraction, cementation and precipitation/crystallization. It is also possible to use electrowinning or electrodialysis for the metal recovery from leached solution whereas graphite is typically lost as part of the leaching residue. (Saleem et al., 2023).

Alternatively, additional mechanical separation methods prior to hydrometallurgical extraction have been recently proposed to enhance the recovery of various material species. For instance, froth flotation has been used in LIBs recycling as a so-called “direct recycling” approach with promising results at laboratory scale. (Huang et al., 2022; Rinne et al., 2023; Rinne et al., 2022; Traore and Kelebek, 2022; Vanderbruggen et al., 2022) Since flotation exploits the differences in wettability, (Wills and Finch, 2015) it is considered a suitable method to separate hydrophobic graphite from hydrophilic lithium-metal oxide cathodes. (Rinne et al., 2023; Vanderbruggen et al., 2022; Sahivirta et al., 2024) In this manner, a graphite-rich fraction is collected as froth concentrate, while a cathode-rich fraction is obtained in the sink. (Traore and Kelebek, 2022) It has been hypothesized that such an approach would benefit the preparation of material feed to leaching processes, as reducing the graphite amount would reduce the throughput and need of filtration capacity in the downstream processes to a significant extent.

One of the biggest challenges to achieve efficient LIB recycling is the strong attachment of insoluble polymeric binder to cathode materials and current collectors. (Dou et al., 2023) (Ali et al., 2022; Doose et al., 2021; Jiang et al., 2023) PDVF is the most common binder used nowadays because it provides mechanical stability and allows the transfer of Li+ ions between the electrodes. (Ali et al., 2022) It has been well documented that, due to its hydrophobic nature, the presence of PVDF on the surface of cathode particles hinders their selective separation with froth flotation. (Vanderbruggen et al., 2022) The PVDF coating also reduces leaching efficiency of cathode particles during the first stage of hydrometallurgical refinement. (Jiang et al., 2024) So far, the two most common approaches to remove the binder have been its dissolution with solvent-based solutions or thermal decomposition. (Dou et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2022) Due to ecological risk of the solvents such as N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) for binder decomposition, pyrolysis is considered a safer and more robust option for the removal of electrolytes, separators, and binders. (Dou et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2019) It has been shown that pyrolysis improves the liberation of active materials, and is considered to be relatively simple and cost-effective. (Zhang et al., 2020; Zhong et al., 2023; Zhong et al., 2019) However, a risk during pyrolysis is the potential evolution of hydrogen fluoride (HF) into the pyrolytic gas, thus requiring additional gas capture using an alkaline solution. (Liu et al., 2019; Tao et al., 2022) Pyrolysis treatment of LIBs has reportedly resulted in an increased separation efficiency in both froth flotation (Zhang et al., 2020) and hydrometallurgical processes. (Jiang et al., 2023; Tao et al., 2022) However, although pyrolysis, flotation and leaching have been widely studied independently as battery recycling steps, the published literature still lacks a systematic study on the efficiency and interactions resulting from their combination in a process. In a previous effort by Vieceli et al. (Vieceli et al., 2022) for instance, it was suggested that the removal of graphite by flotation after a pyrolysis pre-treatment prior to leaching increased the leaching yield of Li and Co whereas it decreased the leaching yields of Ni and Mn by suggesting further research. However, their study conducted pyrolysis at 300 °C whereas the decomposition of binder is expected to be efficient in temperatures approximately 500 °C. (Wang et al., 2023) Furthermore, the effect of pyrolysis on leaching in the absence of flotation was not explored.

Therefore, there is still a need to identify and understand the potential synergetic effects of these processing stages. Thus, this article presents a novel approach by integrating and comparing pyrolysis, flotation, and leaching processes to improve the recycling efficiency of materials, particularly graphite and metals, from battery black mass by promoting the binder removal. To understand the interactions between the pretreatment and extractive stages hereby explored, an experimental campaign was designed by defining the boundary of the recycling system after leaching, where the dissolution of metals can be investigated. It is particularly useful to understand and produce experimental data to establish an optimum scenario between these processes considering that it is gaining relevance to use recycled graphite from spent batteries for the remanufacturing of new LIBs in addition to the recovery of metals of high economic value. It has been recently reported that recycled graphite either from leached residue (Chernyaev et al., 2024) or flotation concentrate (Olutogun et al., 2024) can reach similar performance as the commercial graphite, showing promising results towards the circularity of graphite.

End-of-life battery material powder mixture namely black mass was obtained from an industrial recycling company, where they were disassembled and mechanically treated to remove plastics, Al and Cu. Upon receipt, the material was dried at room temperature to remove the remaining moisture, followed by comminution in a blowing hammer mill (Rivakka, Nipere Oy, Finland) with 4 mm sieve mesh and 3000 rpm angular speed to deagglomerate the particles. The ground black mass was sieved using aperture sizes of 1000, 500, 250 and 125 µm (Retsch AS 300). As will be explained in Section 2.1.2., each size fractions were analyzed to determine its metal content. Only the samples of the finer fraction (<125 µm) of the sieved black mass were pyrolyzed (as explained in Section 2.2.1) to remove volatile hazardous materials and thus allow its safe handling during characterization of particle size distribution (PSD), graphite content and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) imaging.

The PSD of the finest black mass fraction (<125 µm) was investigated by laser diffraction using a Malvern Panalytical Mastersizer 3000 particle size analyzer (Malvern, United Kingdom). A total of 6 measurements were carried out on each sample to determine average values. For comparison, 2 min ultrasonication sample pre-treatment was applied to observe the extent of particle agglomeration.

The elemental analysis characterization of solid samples was performed by dissolution of the samples and using flame atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS, Thermo Scientific iCE 3000, USA) and inductively coupled plasma − optical emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES, Agilent 5900 SVDV ICP-OES, USA) to measure the content of Li, Ni, Co, Mn, Cu, Fe, and Al. The samples were prepared by dissolving 1 g of the material in 40 ml of Aqua Regia (1:3 M ratio of HNO3:HCl) at 70 °C and stirred for 90 min at 300 rpm in a heated stirrer (IKA RT10, Germany).

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, Netzsch STA 449) coupled with evolved gas analysis quadrupole mass spectrometer (MS, EGA QMS 403 Aëolos Quadro) was utilized for the quantification of the remaining organics in the pyrolyzed samples. The experimental procedure previously developed by Gomez-Moreno et al. (Gomez-Moreno et al., 2023) was followed. Briefly, ca. 15 mg of pyrolyzed black mass below 125 µm was placed in an alumina crucible and loaded in the equipment. Helium gas (99.99 % purity, Linde) was introduced in the equipment with a flow of 70 ml/min to create an inert atmosphere while heating the sample from room temperature up to 1000°C using a heating rate of 10 °C/min. MS ion signals were recorded to follow the gas evolution which, together with the mass changes over time, allowed the identification of remaining organics in the pyrolyzed powder samples.

Graphite amount was estimated by combining the results of elemental analysis, pyrolysis and TGA-MS. First, the elemental analysis results (Ni wt-%, Co wt-%, Mn wt-%) were used to estimate the cathode composition in the black mass samples, assuming that the dominant stoichiometries corresponded to LiNiO2, LiCoO2, and LiMnO2. The elemental analysis also gave results on the concentrations of Fe, Al, and Cu, considered metallic contaminants. Then, the mass loss in pyrolysis experiments was used to estimate the amount of volatile organics (%) while TGA-MS quantified the organics residue after pyrolysis. These mass losses were assumed to correspond to species other than graphite. Once the amount of non-graphitic organics and oxygen in LMOs is known, they can be subtracted from the “Others” category, making it is possible to calculate graphite content.

Microscopic image capture was performed using SEM (Tescan Mira3, Brno, Czech Republic). Micrographs were obtained for pyrolyzed samples below < 125 µm and flotation products to compare the effects of processes on the particle morphology. SEM was operated at 15 kV acceleration voltage and images were obtained by back scattered electron (BSE) and secondary electron (SE) detector.

To determine the effect of the processing stages of interest in this work, four different process combinations were studied, represented schematically in Fig. 1 . The different scenarios represent various levels of complexity, starting with only leaching (SC-1), and followed by flotation and leaching (SC-2), pyrolysis and leaching (SC-3) and finally pyrolysis, flotation and leaching (SC-4).

As explained above, the main purpose of pyrolysis was the removal of PVDF binder and any remaining volatile impurity. Pyrolysis was performed in an electrically heated horizontal furnace (Lenton LTF12/50/610 (048)) with a 50 mm diameter and 670 mm length. The total heating length of the furnace was 610 mm. In a typical run, 5 g of sample were placed on a silica crucible and then inserted in the furnace, which was subsequently sealed and flushed with N2 gas (Woikoski N50) at a flow rate of 200 ml/min. The crucible was positioned in the hot zone of the furnace in three steps with 3 min intervals in between (10 cm at a time) to protect the crucible from heat shock. The sample was then treated at a constant temperature of 550 °C for 30 min since these were previously reported to be optimal conditions to improve flotation efficiency. (Zhang et al., 2020) The silica crucible with the pyrolyzed sample was removed from the furnace in three increments with 3 min intervals, similar to its placement into the furnace. After the pyrolysis was completed, the silica crucible with the sample was left to cool down in room temperature and weighted to measure the mass loss caused from the volatilization or decomposition of species.

The off-gas composition analysis during pyrolysis was performed with a gas analyzer (Gasmet, DX4000 FTIR) following the methodology previously proposed by Klemettinen et al. (Klemettinen et al., 2024) N2 was utilized for dilution during the measurements and Calcmet software was used to operate the gas analyzer. Multiple gases were simultaneously monitored, including CO, CO2, N2O, NO, NO2, SO2, NH3, HCl, HF, CH4, C2H4, C3H8, C6H14, and CHOH. The gas line was heated to 180 °C prior to measurements and flushed with N2 before the background measurement for 3 min. The measurements were repeated every 50 s, following a sequence consisting of 30 s gas filling and 20 s for gas measurement.

Batch flotation experiments were performed with a mechanical flotation device (Lab Cell- 60 mm FloatForce mechanism, Outotec) using a 1 l glass cell. 40 g of black mass were dispersed in deionized water directly in the flotation cell. Then, the pulp was mixed for 3 min to ensure proper dispersion using the flotation cell impeller at 1000 rpm agitation speed. After 3 min of mixing, methyl isobutyl carbinol (MIBC) (98 %, Sigma Aldrich) was added as frother reagent up to a concentration of 8 ppm in the pulp, followed by 2 more minutes of conditioning. After the mixing and conditioning time, the impeller speed was decreased to 850 rpm and the air feed valve was opened to allow its feed at a flow rate of 2 l/min. The impeller speed and air flow rate were kept constant during the duration of the flotation experiments. Froth samples were collected for the fractions 0–1, 1–2, 2–4, 4–8 min. Time based collected froth samples and final sink product were collected and vacuum-filtered, followed by drying in a convection oven for 24–48 h at 40 °C.

The leaching experiments were performed at ca. 45 °C with a 1 M stock solution of H2SO4 (95–97 % Merck, Emsure) to have milder leaching conditions. No reductant was added to study the effects of flotation and pyrolysis in leaching and possibly reducing the chemical consumption by eliminating the need of reductant. The actual concentration of stock solution was determined by titrating with 0.5 M NaOH (Titripur, Merck) and 0.1 w/v% Methyl Orange (Schering AG). For each experiment, 10 g of sample were leached in 100 ml leaching solution (i.e., solid liquid (S/L) ratio of 100 g/l). The solution was stirred and heated with a heated stirrer (VWR 7x7 Hotplate-Stirrer, USA) and agitation speed was kept constant at 300 rpm with a magnetic bullet. The duration of each leaching experiment was two hours. To determine leaching kinetics, samples were taken after 15, 30, 60, 90 and 120 min. Additionally, in experiments corresponding to SC-2 and SC-4, samples were taken after 5 and 45 min. Leachate samples were taken with a disposable syringe with a hose inserted into the tip of syringe. The sample was then filtered through a filter paper. For SC-1 and SC-2, the filter paper was Whatman 4, and in SC-3 and SC-4 the filter paper was Whatman 50.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Material characterization and liberation of black mass

ce:section.ce:para: The weight distribution and metal content of black mass in different size fractions are presented in Table 1 . For a more complete picture the distributions of metals at each size fraction, are visualized in Fig. 2 . As seen from Table 1, about 85 % of the black mass was fine particles, smaller than 125 µm. It was also found that this is the size fraction containing the highest concentration of “others,” corresponding to a combination of graphite, oxygen from LMOs and some remaining organic species. The finest size fraction also exhibited the lowest content of Cu and Al, suggesting that this fraction is composed of graphite and LMOs well-liberated from their current collectors during mechanical pretreatment.

As seen in Fig. 2, most of the transition metals associated with cathode materials reported to the < 125 µm fraction, suggesting an efficient liberation from current collector foils. Considering that this is the fraction with the highest mass fraction and content of metals, the subsequent studies on characterization, pyrolysis, flotation and leaching experiments were carried out only for the size fraction below 125 µm.

For a better understanding of the particle size distribution below 125 µm, Fig. 3a and Fig. 3b show the frequency and cumulative PSD of pyrolyzed black mass respectively under normal and ultrasonic stirring conditions. Moreover, the elemental analysis of the pyrolyzed material is also presented in Fig. 3c. It is worth mentioning that there no significant difference in material concentration between non-pyrolyzed (Table 1) and pyrolyzed samples (Fig. 3c) below 125 µm was identified. This reflects a negligible mass of volatile organics or polymer contaminants while providing evidence that the “others” fraction in the < 125 µm particles is mostly associated with graphite and oxygen from LMOs.

The PSD presented in Fig. 3b shows that, in the pyrolyzed < 125 µm fraction, about 90 % of particles are smaller than 45 µm under normal stirring conditions. The mean value of the distribution is positioned at ca. 15 µm, a value close to that of individual cathode and anode particles. This offers another indication of the good liberation of particles in this size fraction, although the presence of larger particles was also observed. When ultrasonic stirring was applied to the same material for 2 min, the d90 particle size shifted to ca. 20 µm. Although speculative, this size reduction may be explained by either the deagglomeration of cathode particles bound by residual or by the dispersion of graphite agglomerates spontaneously produced in water as a result of their hydrophobic nature. In any case, this shows that the remaining agglomerates after pyrolysis do not present a strong mechanical integrity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Liberation of black mass and graphite quantification
ce:section.ce:para: Figs. 4a and b shows the SEM images of pyrolyzed samples with 500 and 1000 magnification respectively. Two distinctive species can be readily identified in the micrographs: dark grey particles representing graphite (representative examples circled in green), and light grey particles corresponding to LMO particles. The LMO examples circled in red show the existence of individual particles and agglomerates partially covered by residual binder, appearing as a darker gray substance of undefined shape, in line with previously reported SEM characterization results. (Salces et al., 2022) While pyrolysis alone may not have completely removed binder, it might still be useful to promote binder degradation and remove undesired volatile and polymeric substances.

The gaseous byproduct species formed throughout the pyrolysis treatment are presented in Fig. 5 . For statistical significance, results of 5 pyrolysis experiments under the same conditions (550 °C-30 min) are presented. In general, the release of gaseous species started after the crucible was placed near to the hot zone of 550 °C (ca. 6 min). The maximum concentration value of all gaseous species was detected as the crucible reached the hot zone (ca. 10 min) with no remaining gases generated after 30 min in the hot zone (ca. 40 min total processing time). The presence of carbon monoxide (CO), methane (CH4) ethane (C2H6), ethylene (C2H4), propane (C3H8) and hexane (C6H14) can be associated with the decomposition of electrolyte since these gases are reportedly formed by the reaction of free Li+ ions with organic carbonates (Liao et al., 2020) such as dimethyl carbonate (DMC-C3H6O3), ethylene carbonate (EC-C3H4O3), ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC-C4H8O3) or diethyl carbonate (DEC-C5H10O3).

The formation of CO, NO and NOx point out to the release of oxygen either from the electrolyte solvent or from the cathode material since pyrolysis was performed under inert gas flow. While the decomposition of cathode material is not expected to be significant below 650 °C, (Gomez-Moreno et al., 2023; Mousa et al., 2023) its reactivity at moderate temperatures can result in the partial reduction of LMOs, as previously shown in the literature. (Huang et al., 2022).

Furthermore, Fig. 6a shows the thermal degradation profile of pyrolyzed black mass samples. In the figures, TG% represents the weight changes of the sample through time and DTG (%/min) is the first derivative of TG %, used to facilitate the identification of degradation stages.

According to DTG (%), the first peak of the weight change occurs around 484 °C and the second peak occurs around 700 °C. In previous studies, (Gomez-Moreno et al., 2023; Mousa et al., 2023) it was seen that the first degradation step in black mass usually occurs between 200–300 °C due to the volatilization of electrolyte and the binder decomposition. The following degradation stage occurs above 650 °C due to the redox decomposition of active materials, resulting in the evolution of CO and CO2. However, since these samples underwent pyrolysis pretreatment before TGA, the mass change was expected to be minimal before cathode decomposition started. Although the SEM images (Fig. 4) show evidence of binder residue, the TGA results suggest that they have a minor contribution to the total sample mass. In addition to that, Fig. 6b shows that no significant gas generation was observed until 700 °C, corroborating that most volatile and polymeric species were removed during pyrolysis.

As mentioned in Section 2.1.4, besides the aim of cathode particles liberation, pyrolysis results were also used for the quantification of graphite. The mass measurements before and after pyrolysis in the finest fraction showed a mass loss of approximately 4 % (AVE: 3.77 % − SD: 0.3 %) based on 34 samples, resulting from the formation of gaseous products of thermal and oxidative degradation. It was assumed that there was negligible cathode decomposition at temperatures lower than 550 °C and thus the mass lost during pyrolysis represented the amount of electrolyte and organic species in the sample. Using the ICP-OES analyses of Ni, Co and Mn content, the mass of oxygen corresponding to stoichiometrical composition of LMOs with these elements was calculated. Thus, after subtracting the oxygen contribution from the “Others” fraction in pyrolyzed samples, the remaining fraction can be reasonably assumed to be graphite. Table 2 shows the mass compositions of the black mass samples calculated from the various characterization methods used in this work.

The recovery of various species throughout the two flotation scenarios (i.e., without and with pyrolysis) are presented in the Fig. 7 . It was found that, in the absence of pyrolysis, the recovery of LMOs in the froth exceeded 40 % after 8 min, clearly contaminating what was expected to be a graphite-rich fraction. When pyrolysis was applied, the LMOs recovery in the froth decreased below 20 %. Admittedly, pyrolysis also resulted in a reduction of graphite recovery in the froth although this was comparatively moderate. It is worth noting that the experiments were performed using only frother as a flotation reagent and thus, there may be room for optimization of graphite recovery, perhaps using surface-modifying additives (e.g., collectors). This optimization is however considered outside the scope of the present work. Despite the suboptimal conditions used, more than 50 % of graphite was collected from the black mass during flotation.

Although pyrolysis reduced the recovery of graphite, its purity in the froth fraction increased from ca. 60 to 80 wt-%, as shown in Fig. 7 (c and d). Furthermore, despite the increase in the recovery values over time, the cumulative grade values mostly stayed stable. These results are well in line with previously published studies suggesting that binder removal results in an improved flotation performance. (Zhang et al., 2021).

The froth fractions of the flotation from the pyrolyzed material were also analyzed with SEM to better understand the behavior of the cathode material. Fig. 8 shows that, despite the evident enrichment of graphite in the froth phase, cathode particles were invariably present. As seen from Fig. 8, most of the recovered cathode particles are only a few microns in size (blue circles). A few larger cathode particles (orange circle) were also observed, some of which appear to remain coated with residual binder. The existence of larger cathode particles in Fig. 8 shows that the unselective recovery of cathode materials in the froth phase can be attributed to either particle entrainment or an induced hydrophobicity by the residual binder on the cathode particles. While the benefits of pyrolysis to improve graphite purity are demonstrated in Fig. 7, the images in Fig. 8 provide suggestions to further improve separation efficiency. Additional efforts to remove binder such as attrition (Vanderbruggen et al., 2022), ultrasonic treatment (Zhang et al., 2018) or two-stage pyrolysis (Zhu and Chen, 2020) could be considered. Furthermore, to reduce the entrainment of fine cathode material crystals, selective agglomeration is a promising alternative. (Rinne et al., 2023).

The material content of the leach feed in different scenarios is given in Table 3 . It is important to note that the difference in cathode material content differs between scenarios due to the various pretreatment methods used prior to leaching. As expected, the highest cathode material content in the feed is that of scenario SC-4 in which a more intensive pre-treatment resulted in the removal of organic species by pyrolysis and more than 50 % graphite by flotation.

The extraction rates of the leaching experiments for the different leaching feed samples (Table 3) can be seen from Fig. 9 . It was observed that the metals were dissolved mainly during the first 15 min of leaching, independently from the previous process steps. In the case of the cathode active components, in all scenarios, high extraction rates (above 90 %) for Li were observed. The leaching rates for Li and Ni were calculated to be slightly above 100 % in SC-3. While this is not physically possible, it is likely associated with experimental error and due to the heterogeneous nature of industrially produced black mass, as previously reported in the literature (Chernyaev et al., 2024). Extraction rates for Co, Mn and Ni were lowest in SC-1 (40 %) where no pre-treatment on the black mass was performed. These extraction rates were in alignment with previous research where the leaching rate of Co, Mn and Ni from pure NMC particles are nearly 40 % in the absence of any reductants. (Zou et al., 2024) When only flotation was applied (i.e., SC-2), the extraction rates for Co and Ni were increased nearly 5–10 %. Since there were not any addition of chemicals such as collectors that can chemically react with graphite or lithium oxide particles during flotation, such increase might be due to the partial removal of graphite or a better deagglomeration of cathode particles aided by agitation during flotation. Deagglomeration would increase the free surface areas of particles, causing a slightly better leaching efficiency.

Interestingly, it was not possible to observe the same increase in the leaching efficiency of Mn. This is however a relative measure and might be associated with the higher content of Mn in the leach feed of SC-2 compared to SC-1. Indeed, when the metal dissolution is evaluated in terms of concentration in g/L (Fig. 10 ), the total mass of leached Mn was significantly higher in SC-2 compared to SC-1. The dissolved metal concentrations were increased from 2.44 g/L to 3.13 g/L for Co, from 7.24 g/L to 9.29 g/L for Ni and from 2.26 g/L to 3.02 g/L for Mn at the end of 2 h.

Furthermore, it was observed that the leaching rate for Cu, Fe and Al were also increased by the application of flotation. It is possible that the deagglomeration of the particles first increased the leaching rates of Cu, Fe and Al, and then consequently, the leaching rates of Co and Ni were increased by using the electrons released by the dissolution of Cu and Al. It has been previously reported that the Cu and Al residues found in industrial black mass promote increase the leaching rates of the transition metals. (Chernyaev et al., 2021; Vieceli et al., 2023) This is attributed to their lower reduction potentials (Cu = 0.334 V; Al = -1.68 V) when compared to battery cathode metal oxides such as LCO or NMC (LCO = 2.15 V). The leaching reactions can be as follows (Partinen et al., 2024)

While Eqs. (1) and (2) present reactions with a cathode type LCO, based on the literature, it is hypothesized that these oxidation and reduction reactions are similar for other chemistries such as NMC. (Partinen et al., 2024) Nevertheless, it is seen from Table 3 that the amount of Cu and Al was also small to reduce all the transition metals in all scenarios. The required electron needed for the reduction of transition metals was calculated between 0.5–0.7 M depending on the scenario whereas Cu and Al were only available in 0.01–0.02 M and 0.1–0.2 M respectively. Therefore, it is not expected to have the leaching yields above 50 % with the low ratios of available reductant to transition metal (SC-1: 0.41, SC-2: 0.18, SC-3, 0.42, SC-4:0.38).

Despite there was not enough available reductant in the black mass, the concentration of leached metals was further increased by the application of pyrolysis, according to result from both SC-3 and SC-4. This improvement can be partly associated by the removal of the binder, increasing the free surface area of the cathode material from the physical aspect. However, a more important effect might be the chemical reduction of metal oxides into pure metallic species at the cathode surfaces, increasing the infiltration of acid through the particles and easing their extraction. The positive impact on the extraction rates by pyrolysis has also been suggested in previous research available in the literature. (Petranikova et al., 2022) This effect can be due to the reduction of cathode material in pyrolysis in the presence of CO (Fig. 5) evolved from the decomposition of electrolyte residues. The possible species can be different oxides form of Mn (Mn3O4), Co (Co3O4) and Ni (NiO) in addition to reduced lithium metal oxides (LiMn2O4, LiNiO, LiCoO). (Petranikova et al., 2022).

Figs. 9 and 10 show that leaching efficiency can be further improved by using flotation pre-treatment after pyrolysis despite the available in situ reductant to transition metal oxide ratio is lower in SC-4 compared to SC-3. While the effect of flotation to increase leaching yields has been largely speculated, this work objectively demonstrates the benefit of removing graphite in a significant extent prior to leaching. When flotation was applied in SC-4, there was a further increase on the recovery of Co and Ni (5–10 %) but not for Mn, an analogous behavior to the previously discussed scenarios without pyrolysis (SC-2 vis-à-vis SC-1). When the dissolved metal concentrations were evaluated, it was again possible to observe the increase for total mass of Mn extracted. Based on the results shown in Fig. 10, a positive impact of flotation can be stated, resulting in a significant increase of dissolved transition metals concentration in 2 h from 5.5 g/L to 6.9 g/L for Co, from 5.6 g/L to 7.4 g/L for Mn and from 13.7 g/L to 19.8 g/L for Ni.

On the other hand, the leaching rates for the impurity elements Fe, Al and Cu were similar after pyrolysis applied for SC-3 and SC-4 whereas the rates were higher in SC-3 compared to SC-1. One possible explanation for this behavior can be that the chemical changes during pyrolysis were dominant to the physical changes that occurred in flotation. After pyrolysis, the formation of Cu oxides (CuO) (Petranikova et al., 2022) might increase the dissolution rate of Cu during the leaching whereas the binder removal might increase the access to Al and Fe resulting in slightly higher leaching efficiencies.

The only exception of increased metal dissolution was for Li. This might be an unexpected consequence of the dissolution of Li in water during flotation, as previously shown by Salces et al. (Salces et al., 2022) Indeed, a lower Li content was observed in the leach feed of SC-4 compared to other scenarios (Table 3), which likely resulted in lower Li concentration in the pregnant solution. It is possible that thermal treatments such as pyrolysis or sulfation roasting enhance the leaching efficiency of Li in water. (Saleem et al., 2025; Biswas et al., 2023) It should be also noted that the dissolution of Li in flotation makes the cathode particle less stable after flotation by opening the crystal lattice of metal oxides to the acidic solution and increase the leaching rates, and this might be one of the reasons for slightly improved leaching rates in SC-2 and SC-4.

In fact, Fig. 10 shows that the dissolution efficiency of leaching solution (1 M H2SO4) is systematically increased by flotation and pyrolysis when no externally added reducing agent is used during leaching. This is due to the possible reduction of LMOs already occurred in pyrolysis. (Petranikova et al., 2022) Therefore, it can be claimed that the surface transformations driven by pyrolysis unexpectedly aids in the separation of species both during flotation and leaching. In combination with a pre-treatment stage such as flotation for the removal of graphite, the leaching efficiency of metals is increased. Also, this positive impact of flotation on leaching was performed in the absence of external reductants, whereas substantially higher extractions (up to 100 % for Ni, Mn, Co and Li) were reported in the literature when e.g·H2O2 is used in the leaching process in a more acidic environment with a higher content of Cu and Al in the black mass. (Vieceli et al., 2023) Nevertheless, the experimental results in this study with a black mass containing minimal amount of Al and Cu explain why the combination of pyrolysis and flotation is advantageous for the efficient leaching of battery materials. Admittedly, the leaching efficiencies achieved (Fig. 9) are not yet high enough to meet EU battery regulation targets (Co, Ni > 95 %). Therefore, further studies on the use of additional reductant may be needed to reach an economically viable industrial production.

The results in this study pointed out the advantages of combining pyrolysis, flotation and leaching for LIBs recycling while revealing several dimensions to investigate for future research. Firstly, while flotation helps to increase the leaching throughput in operation by removing graphite, it also caused the loss of cathode materials in the flotation froth phase, even after removal of contaminants with pyrolysis. The characterization of float and sink products suggest at least two avenues of research worth exploring for future flotation studies: i) the use of secondary mechanical treatments for further binder detachment, and ii) selective flocculation of cathode microparticles. Considering the recovery rate for graphite is not yet high enough to support its circularity in this work, a combined improved liberation and reduced cathode entrainment would lead to an increased graphite recovery while minimizing metal losses in flotation concentrate offering more economically viable options. Although optimization of flotation was considered outside of the scope of this work, some efforts showing promising results can be found in the literature. (Vanderbruggen et al., 2022; Rinne et al., 2024) Furthermore, it was demonstrated that the combination of pyrolysis and flotation resulted in materials with suitable properties as leach feed. Using this pre-treatment route, some reduction is suggested to occur already during the pyrolysis decreasing the need of reductants in the acid leaching media, indicating a potential decrease in the chemical consumption during leaching to be further investigated. Moreover, the use of additional or in-situ reductants requires increased efforts for their removal in the downstream purification stages allowing pyrolysis to be considered as an alternative pre-treatment method to improve leaching efficiency without additional reductant usage in leaching. On the other hand, pyrolysis is an energy intensive process that will likely increase the overall energy consumption of the whole process. Furthermore, pyrolysis is also associated with the generation of hazardous gases. This has prompted the exploration of alternative ways of binder removal, e.g., with the use of solvents. (Golmohammadzadeh et al., 2023; Henderson et al., 2025) While some positive results have been reported with, these alternatives are still in the early stages and are yet to be proven at a larger scale. Therefore, it also becomes vital to evaluate the recycling processes from multiple dimensions to point out the best scenario. Further analysis comparing the performance of these recycling routes using parameters of mass and energy preservation, and environmental impact is another interesting avenue for future studies.

The present study applied a systematic approach to study the effects of pyrolysis, flotation and leaching on battery materials and their integration for a more efficient recovery of graphite and metals. It was seen that pyrolysis helped to increase the efficiency of both flotation and leaching processes. After the application of pyrolysis before flotation, the graphite grade in the graphite concentrate was increased from 60 to 80 % despite graphite recovery was decreased from 60 to 50 %. Simultaneously, LMO losses into the froth concentrate decreased from 20 to 10 % resulting in an enriched cathode stream. Moreover, pyrolysis in the current study helped to increase the leaching yield of the metals from 40 to > 80 %, due to the partial reduction of cathode active material and binder removal, allowing better physical access to the metallic species and increased leaching of transition metals without using additional reductants. However, the published literature shows that similar increase in the transition metal yields can be consistently achieved in a sole leaching unit process by the use of additional or in-situ reductants. Furthermore, the combination of pyrolysis and flotation resulted in a significant improvement on the dissolved metal concentrations between for all of metals, except Li. The lower Li-leaching yields were associated to its dissolution in flotation.
